Ethics, Engineering, and Economics

Data visualization showing ethical engineering practice and my responsibility to the engineering practice

Andrew Robertson (30145749) March 10, 2026
Circular Design Research [2]
This visualization within the doughnut (representing the space where society can thrive sustainably) highlights principles of the circular economy. Based on Circular Design Research in The Netherlands [2], the green segments represent six circular design strategies that can guide how engineers develop products and systems. Rather than following a traditional linear "take-make-dispose" model, these strategies emphasize keeping materials in use longer, designing for reuse or repair, and reducing waste from the start. From an ethical engineering perspective, these approaches demonstrate how design decisions directly influence environmental sustainability and resource use. As I move toward becoming an engineer, this reinforces my responsibility to consider the full lifecycle of the systems I help create and to prioritize designs that are regenerative and socially responsible rather than simply efficient or profitable.
Seven Ways to think like a 21st Economist [1]
The outer ring represents the seven principles proposed by Kate Raworth for rethinking modern economics. Together they encourage a shift away from growth-centered thinking (outside of the doughnut) toward systems that balance social well-being with environmental limits. For engineers, these ideas highlight the importance of systems thinking and long-term responsibility when designing technologies that influence both society and the planet. As future professionals, this perspective reminds us that engineering decisions should contribute to economies that help communities thrive while remaining within Earth's ecological boundaries.
Distributive Justice [4]
Inside the doughnut, a bar chart based on data from Environmental Hazards [4] shows the demographic characteristics of communities within a 25-mile radius of SpaceX's launch site. As the article notes, "SpaceX's operations in the Rio Grande Valley have sparked a growing debate about the balance between technological innovation and environmental and social responsibility" [4]. With local community data shown in dark red and Texas statewide averages in lighter red, the chart reveals large gaps in several indicators, including minority status and poverty. This pattern reflects broader concerns about environmental racism, where industrial or hazardous infrastructure is often located near vulnerable communities with less political power. As discussed in John Oliver's Environmental Racism: Last Week Tonight segment [5], such patterns can contribute to long-term health disparities and reduced life expectancy in affected neighborhoods. Within the doughnut framework, this highlights how some communities still fall short of basic social foundations. From an ethical engineering perspective, it underscores the responsibility engineers have to consider not only technical performance but also the social and environmental impacts of projects on nearby communities. Especially those already facing economic and political disadvantages.

Reflection

Inspired by the Doughnut Economics discussed in class and the doughnut charts seen [3], I wanted to create a visualization that connects circular economy principles with real-world ethical challenges in engineering. The doughnut represents a space where society can thrive by meeting human needs while remaining within ecological limits. When exploring the supporting resources, I was particularly struck by the contrast between this ideal framework and the realities highlighted in the SpaceX case study [4]. While SpaceX represents major technological innovation, its launch facilities are located in a region with high poverty and minority populations, raising concerns about environmental justice and the unequal distribution of risk. Similar issues arose in John Olivers Environmental Racism [5] and I see this isn't a "one off" issue.

These examples helped me recognize that engineering decisions can never be purely technical. Choices about where technologies are built, how systems are designed, and who benefits from them can have significant social and environmental consequences. As engineers, we often focus on efficiency, performance, and innovation, but this assignment reinforced that ethical engineering practice must also consider fairness, sustainability, and the well-being of affected communities. Frameworks such as Doughnut Economics and circular design demonstrate that engineers can actively shape systems that distribute benefits more fairly and reduce environmental harm. This directly relates to Rule of Conduct 2, which requires engineers to "perform services only in areas of their competence and with consideration for the environment, health, and safety of the public" [6].

As a future electrical engineer, this perspective influences how I think about my own professional responsibility. For example, when designing electronic systems, manufacturing processes, or infrastructure, I can make decisions that reduce waste, improve repairability, and minimize environmental impact through circular design strategies. Similarly, when involved in large infrastructure or industrial projects, I must consider how my work may affect nearby communities, particularly those that are already socially or economically vulnerable. Recognizing these impacts is an important part of professional accountability, and aligns with APEGA Rule of Conduct 5, which states that engineers shall "not practice in a manner that discriminates on the basis of race, religion, national origin, or social class" and must respect human rights [6]. The disproportionate impact on minority and low-income communities highlighted in both the SpaceX case and environmental racism examples shows why this rule is essential to ethical practice.

Going forward, I want to apply these principles by prioritizing designs that consider the entire lifecycle of a technology and the broader systems it interacts with. Whether working in electronics, energy systems, or emerging technologies, I hope to contribute to projects that align innovation with social responsibility and environmental stewardship. This assignment reminded me that engineers play an important role in shaping the systems that society depends on, and that ethical engineering practice requires actively working toward technologies that support both people and the planet.

References

[1] DEAL Team, "Get Animated! Introducing the Seven Ways," doughnuteconomics.org, Jul. 09, 2020. https://doughnuteconomics.org/tools/get-animated-introducing-the-seven-ways (accessed Mar. 08, 2026).

[2] M. den Hollander, M. Idema, and P. Joore, Circular Design Research in The Netherlands. TU Delft OPEN Publishing, 2024.

[3] F. Surjadjaja, "Doughnut Economy by Country," doughnut-economy-fxs7576.netlify.app, Aug. 27, 2022. https://doughnut-economy-fxs7576.netlify.app (accessed Mar. 09, 2026).

[4] E. Garcia, D. Kyne, W. Donner, and A. Lomeli, "Understanding the new frontiers of risk, social vulnerability, and environmental justice in space exploration in the Rio Grande Valley," Environmental Hazards, Feb. 2025, doi: https://doi.org/10.1080/17477891.2025.245996.

[5] J. Oliver, "Environmental Racism: Last Week Tonight with John Oliver (HBO)," www.youtube.com, May 02, 2022. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-v0XiUQlRLw (accessed Mar. 10, 2026).

[6] "APEGA Rules of Conduct," APEGA, March 2023. Accessed: Mar. 11, 2026. [Online]. Available: https://www.apega.ca/docs/default-source/pdfs/standards-guidelines/code-of-ethics-5-rules-of-conduct-reference-guide-march-2023.pdf?sfvrsn=ef0d8c00_3
